roots.title: Introduction to Modeling and Regression Analysis
roots.summary: This segment introduces the modeling phase of Data 100, focusing on simple linear regression (SLR) and the significance of models in data science. It outlines future lecture plans, including mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation, while reviewing concepts from Data 8. A model is defined as an idealized representation summarizing trends with limitations, illustrated by an example of an object's fall under gravity. The discussion highlights modeling assumptions, the simplification of complex dynamics, and the need for good sample representation. Reasons for building models include clarifying variable relationships and improving prediction accuracy. Causal inference is introduced to differentiate variable relationships from correlation, emphasizing the balance between interpretability and accuracy. Model types are categorized into deterministic and probabilistic models, with examples provided. Key regression analysis concepts are covered, including the definition and calculation of the intercept.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Regression Analysis
children.summary: The segment introduces the modeling phase of Data 100, focusing on simple linear regression (SLR) and the importance of models in data science. It outlines future lecture plans, including mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation, while reviewing concepts from Data 8. A model is defined as an idealized representation summarizing trends with acknowledged limitations, illustrated by an example of an object's fall under gravity. The discussion emphasizes modeling assumptions, the simplification of complex dynamics, and the necessity of good sample representation. Reasons for building models include clarifying variable relationships and enhancing prediction accuracy. Causal inference is introduced to understand variable relationships, distinguishing it from correlation, with a focus on the balance between interpretability and accuracy. Model types are categorized into deterministic and probabilistic models, with examples provided. Key concepts in regression analysis are also covered, including the definition and calculation of the intercept.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces the modeling phase of Data 100, emphasizing simple linear regression (SLR) and the significance of models in data science. It outlines plans for future lectures, including mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation, alongside a review of concepts from Data 8. A model is defined as an idealized representation that summarizes trends while acknowledging limitations. An example of an object's fall under gravity illustrates how real-world factors can affect model accuracy. The discussion includes modeling assumptions, highlighting that models simplify complex dynamics and rely on the assumption of good sample representation from population data. The reasons for building models are explained: to clarify relationships between variables and to enhance prediction accuracy, especially with large datasets.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The lecture introduces the modeling phase of Data 100, focusing on simple linear regression (SLR) and the importance of models. The instructor outlines plans for upcoming lectures, which will involve mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation. A review of concepts from Data 8 will precede the exploration of modeling techniques.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling in Data Science
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of a model in data science, defining it as an idealized representation of a system that aims to summarize trends while acknowledging that it may not capture all details or noise. An example provided is the modeling of an object's fall under constant gravity, illustrating that while the standard gravity value is 9.81 m/s², real-world factors like elevation and air resistance can affect accuracy. Additionally, a roadmap for upcoming lectures on modeling is outlined, including topics such as simple linear regression and multiple linear regression.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And we're going to go a little bit deeper into more advanced models up to the point that in the last lecture of the regression sequence, we're going to talk about multiple regression. So what happens when you have multiple different attributes that you want to model and you want to combine them into predicting one variable? So this is a roadmap for today. We're going to start by talking about what is a model. Most of you might know what is a model or like you've used the term that there is a machine learning model and whatnot before, but just to tell you what a model is from our perspective in data 100 is that we always think about a model as an idealized representation of a system. So we say that it's an idealized representation of the system because it's not always accurate, we're not always considering all the details and all different features and all the noise that's involved in the system, but we're trying to actually have a model that kind of simulates or summarizes the trend that's happening in a system as accurately as possible. So an example of that could be modeling the fall of an object, an Earth subject to constant gravity that we always think about that as the gravity as being 9.81 meter per second square, but we all know that depending on the elevation, depending on the air resistance, this number is not accurate. A model is an idealized representation of a system.
1380664
Example:
We model the fall of an object on Earth as subject to a constant
acceleration of 9.81 m/s2 due to gravity.
While this describes the behavior of our system, it is merely
an approximation.
It doesn't account for the effects of air resistance, local
variations in gravity, etc.
But in practice, it's accurate enough to be useful!

children.title: Modeling Assumptions and Their Utility
children.summary: Models are idealized representations of systems that simplify complex dynamics for usability. In data 100, the focus will be on modeling population data despite not having complete population information, relying on the assumption that the dataset is a good sample. Not all data points will align with the model, representing noise that is often excluded from direct modeling. The saying by George Box highlights that while all models are wrong, some can still be useful, which reflects the approach taken in the course.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's not going to be a constant model or a constant valley that we can use in order to model the gravity. So this is an example that we simplify assumptions, so that we have a model that's kind of roughly accurate. It's usable. It's simplifying all the dynamics of the, the dynamics in the underlying nature of the system that we have. And that's actually what we're going to do in data 100 as well. Because what you've seen in the previous lecture is that we want to model for population, but we don't have the population. We're trying to simplify assumptions, say that okay, we're sampling. Our dataset is a good sample and is a good representation of the population. And now we're going to do the modeling. And not all of our data points are going to follow the trend that we're going to to model. So those are kind of the noises that we're trying to also avoid or not directly model. So there's a famous saying by George Box that says essentially all mothers are wrung but some are useful, which is exactly what you should expect from all the modeling work that we'll be doing in data 100 as well. children.title: Reasons for Building Models in Data Science
children.summary: Models are built for two main reasons: first, to explain complex phenomena, such as the relationship between parents' and children's average heights, and to create simple, interpretable models that clarify variable relationships. Second, to achieve high accuracy in predictions, especially when working with large datasets, models may incorporate numerous parameters, such as in deep learning architectures, to enhance predictive accuracy.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: we want to build models. And two of these reasons you might see them in data 100 this semester. Reason one is that there's a complex phenomenon that we want to actually model. So for example, we want to figure out our parents average heights related to chillers average heights. So there's like all of those complex relationships that we can study directly, but we can actually collect a bunch of data and then model based on or create a model based on those assumptions or those data points that we have. So in this case, what we would say is that the concept that we're modeling is very complex. What we want to have is a very simple model that is also interpretable, but it's also at the same time reasonably accurate. We don't want to try and random kind of estimations of the values that we want. The second reason is that We might want to have really accurate productions we have a good amount of data we want to make sure that we have very good accurate productions and in doing so what we will do is we'll throw in multiple different parameters not multiple but millions of parameters into a deep learning architecture for example in order to have a very accurate production. children.title: Black Box Models and Spam Detection Project
children.summary: Black box models are characterized by numerous parameters that make it difficult to identify relationships and interactions between them; however, they tend to provide more accurate predictions than simpler, interpretable models. An example is spam email detection, which will be a project in Data 100, where students will classify emails as spam or not based on subject lines and body text. The importance of accuracy in this context is emphasized to avoid misclassifying important emails.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what's happening when we want to use these black box models, we call them black box models because there's millions of different parameters. It's not easily, we cannot easily identify what's the relationship between them or what is the interaction between different parameters that is bits out, the actual estimation that we have at the end. But what we know is that over the population or over the sample that we have, we would say that it's accurate enough that we can actually use it. And it's more accurate than most of the interpretable models or simple models that we've seen in the first case. So examples could be if you want to have a spam email detection. So this is kind of a project that you will do in the last project of data 100. We're going to give you emails with subject line, with the text in the body, and then you will be classifying emails into class of a spam versus not a spam. What you want is you really want to be accurate here. You don't want to have a spam email landing in your inbox or vice versa, missing an important email and moving that into your spam folder. children.title: Causal Inference and Model Types in Data Science
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of model building in data science, emphasizing the need for accurate predictions even at the cost of interpretability. Causal inference is introduced as a method to understand variable relationships, distinguishing it from mere correlation. The balance between interpretability and accuracy is highlighted, with an ideal model being both interpretable and accurate. The types of models are categorized into deterministic (mechanistic) and probabilistic models, with deterministic models providing clear relationships without involving probability, exemplified by historical laws like Kepler's and Newton's. Probabilistic models, on the other hand, incorporate randomness and are useful in classification tasks, illustrated by an example of studying brain waves to assess anxiety.

children.title: Causal Inference and Model Building Reasons
children.summary: The importance of model building is highlighted, focusing on making accurate predictions, even if it results in an uninterpretable model. Causal inference is introduced as a more advanced topic, relevant for understanding relationships between variables, such as determining if smoking causes lung cancer rather than just correlating the two. The discussion emphasizes that in earlier stages of modeling, the goal is to find a function mapping inputs to outputs, while causal inference seeks to establish whether a variable causes the output.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is very important. In these cases, we would say that the reason that we want to do the model is to have a very accurate prediction, even if it comes at the cost of the model, not being interpretable. Reason tree is a reason that we will not go over in data 100, but that's a topic for more advanced courses, some stats courses or data 102 covers causal inference. So for example, if you want to figure out if a smoking causes cancers, not correlation between a smoking and a person having lung cancer, but figuring out the causation between causation relationship between a variable and another variable or a response output, in those cases, we do causal inference. Again, not the topic for data 100, but just so that you know that everything that we're modeling in a stage one and two, it's mostly around finding a function that maps input to an output. But what we're looking at in the recent tree is to actually have a causal inference kind of argument and figuring out if a variable not only explains the output, but also causes the output. children.title: Importance of Interpretability and Accuracy in Modeling
children.summary: Interpretability and accuracy are crucial in data science, and a balance between the two is sought. While some models may lack interpretability, the ideal scenario is to have an interpretable model that remains sufficiently accurate. Common model types include mechanistic or deterministic models, which follow a non-probabilistic approach based on empirical measurements, exemplified by historical laws such as Kepler's and Newton's. Additionally, models serve three primary purposes: explaining complex phenomena, making accurate predictions about unseen data, and inferring causal relationships.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Again, others go pernata 100. Throughout the explanation of this reason one, two, three, I tried to say that interpretability is important and also accuracy is important. So what we'll try to do generally as data scientists is to strike the balance between interpretability and accuracy. So you really don't want to have a model, I mean, in some cases, we do have models that are not interpretable, but the best case scenario would be that If you have an intrapartibled model that is at the same time is accurate enough for you to use as well. So, common types of models. There are mechanistic models or deterministic models. These are models that generally follow a non-probabilistic view, meaning that there is a study or there are measurements, mechanistic measurements that we would do, such as, for example, the way that people arrived at Kepler's, for the law of planetary motion or Newton's law. children.title: Deterministic vs. Probabilistic Models in Research
children.summary: Models in research can be categorized as deterministic or probabilistic. Deterministic models, also known as mechanistic models, do not involve probability; they establish clear relationships between measured variables. In contrast, probabilistic models account for randomness and are often used in classification tasks. An example discussed involves studying brain waves to assess anxiety levels, highlighting how these random processes can be modeled to understand their interactions and effects, such as anxiety or seizures. Additionally, common deterministic models include Kepler's Third Law of Planetary Motion and Newton's Laws of motion and gravitation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And based on those measurements, they built a relationship between multiple different variables and they built those models. We call these models mechanistic or deterministic models. We call them deterministic because there is no probability involved. When they measure, they measure it, the values is there, and then they measure multiple different variables and they basically look at the relationship between them. There are models that are probabilistic. You will see one example of a probabilistic model towards the end of data 100, and mostly we will see probabilistic models when we want to do classification or identification of a certain task. So an example of that, I brought it here from one of my papers that is the work of my PhD student looking into studying brain waves in order to figure out the anxiety levels of subjects. So brain waves by themselves are random processes that you're modeling. There are probabilistic models that basically you will try to study and figure out how they travel in brain and how they interact with each other and how would they concentrate in a certain area so that it causes anxiety or it causes, for example, seizure. So an example of that, I brought it here from one of my papers that is the work of my PhD student looking into studying brain waves in order to figure out the anxiety levels of subjects. So brain waves by themselves are random processes that you're modeling. There are probabilistic models that basically you will try to study and figure out how they travel in brain and how they interact with each other and how would they concentrate in a certain area so that it causes anxiety or it causes, for example, seizure.

children.title: Probabilistic Models and Regression Analysis
children.summary: The discussion focuses on modeling techniques to predict seizure onset using probabilistic models that assess the likelihood of a patient experiencing a seizure based on recorded brain waves. It contrasts mechanistic models, which do not involve probability, with probabilistic models that account for randomness. The segment introduces simple linear regression as a foundational concept, outlining the plan to derive the regression line equation and review related concepts from Data 8.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is kind of the modeling that they would do in order to predict whether someone is going to have an onset of seizure in future or not just looking at those probabilistic models. And then you will basically have a probabilistic model that will say that with this specific likelihood, this subject that we had to recording up their brain waves over the last, like for example, seven days is going to have seizure in, for example, in the next 10 minutes. And if that probability is high enough then basically in patient facilities, they'll try to monitor that patient more accurately or more closely. So this is the kind of different categories of models that we're looking at. Mechanistic or deterministic, there's no probability involved and probabilistic models that again you will see later on in data 100. What we're going to do in data 100 right now as a starting point, just to get warmed up as to take a look at simple linear regression and what you'd see in data 8. So we're going to try go over regression line and the definition for regression line and actually derive the equation for regression line that you'd seen. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Introduction to Objective Functions and Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces the concept of objective functions and emphasizes the importance of derivatives in optimization. It reviews a regression line example from a previous course, illustrating how it predicts the number of characters in chapters of 'Little Women' based on punctuation. The regression line is defined as the unique straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation, with the slope represented as the correlation coefficient multiplied by the standard deviations of the variables involved.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But this time we're going to actually use a little bit of calculus in this lecture to just get warm up because you will need to get comfortable with actually doing derivatives and calculating or optimizing an objective function and whatnot. I'm going to tell you what objective function is by the end of today's lecture. So let's dive in. You've seen this regression line from before, right? So you've seen that in data aid, this was a famous plot that you had that was predicting on different chapters of little women novel that using a number of periods, period punctuations in a chapter, it was trying to predict the number of characters that would appear in that specific chapter. You would see that it tries to modulate using regression line and then we're going to take a look at the equations for that regression line and HUD data aid arrived at that regression line. You also see that in data aid, we defined a slope as correlation or R times the standard deviation of Y over the standard deviation of X. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Regression Analysis: Intercepts, Residuals, and Predictions
children.summary: The segment covers key concepts in regression analysis, including the definition of the intercept as the Y value when X equals zero, and its calculation as the average of Y minus the slope times the average of X. It emphasizes understanding over memorization, stating that knowing two key model aspects allows derivation of equations. Predictions are made using the regression equation, which combines the intercept and the slope multiplied by the value. Residuals, defined as the difference between observed and estimated values, are crucial for model accuracy, with the goal of minimizing them across the dataset. The regression line is described as the unique line that minimizes mean squared error, typically passing through the center of data points, balancing positive and negative residuals. The segment also touches on the correlation concept.

children.title: Understanding Regression Estimation and Intercepts
children.summary: The segment discusses the Y and X dimensions in regression analysis, defining the intercept as the Y value when X equals zero, and explaining that it can be calculated as the average of Y minus the slope times the average of X. It emphasizes that memorization of equations is unnecessary, as understanding two key aspects of a model allows for derivation of all equations. Additionally, it covers regression estimation, where predictions are made by plugging values into the regression equation, which consists of the intercept plus the slope multiplied by the value. The concept of the regression line is introduced, defined as the unique line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation.

Audio: This is your Y dimension and this is your X dimension and correlation. I'm going to formula define it in a sec. An intercept, we talked about intercept or the point that when X is equal to zero, your Y value is non-zero probably, or that's called Y intercept or intercept was defined as average of Y minus slope times average of X. And we're going to see why that's the case. So you don't need to actually memorize all of these equations anymore. You just need to know two things about a model in order to drive all the equations yourself. So you're going to be released from memorizing kind of task from this point in time. We also talked about regression estimation. So regression estimation is that, okay, you have a model or estimation on any model, per se, is that we have a model and we're gonna just plug in the value that we wanna do prediction on into the equation and the equation was intercept plus slope times the value and then that would give you the regression estimation. children.title: Understanding Residuals in Data Modeling
children.summary: Residuals are defined as the difference between observed values and estimated values in data modeling. The goal is to minimize these residuals throughout the dataset, ensuring that the model accurately fits the data points. Some residuals may be positive while others are negative, indicating the degree of prediction error. The regression line is highlighted as the unique line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation, with the formula for regression estimates provided: intercept plus slope multiplied by x. This segment emphasizes the importance of understanding and analyzing residuals to improve model accuracy.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There is a concept of residual that we're going to play a lot with and residual is the fine as observed value minus the estimated value. Okay, so the residuals are actually shown here on the plot. This is a residual. This is a residual. This is a residual and so on and so forth. What we're trying to do in data 100 and true out the modeling is that we're looking at the residuals that we're accumulating true out the entire data set and we're trying to minimize the amount of residual and I'm going to define what amount of residual means formally using a function mathematical function later on in today's lecture, but just know that if a point is not on the line, that's an imperfect prediction and we're going to measure how imperfect it is and we're going to try to make sure that the model that we're using is going to be a real we're fitting to the data is passing through all the data points as accurately as possible. One observation here is that some residuals are positive, some residuals are negative. What you're seeing here is that, for example, for this specific data point, the estimation is exactly this, sorry, the actual value is this value right here on the y-axis. children.title: Understanding Residuals and Regression Line
children.summary: The estimated value is represented by projecting a point onto the regression line, with the predicted value denoted as 'hat.' Residuals are defined as the difference between observed values and predicted values, resulting in positive residuals for points above the line and negative residuals for points below it. This leads to the conclusion that the regression line typically passes through the center of the data points, balancing positive and negative residuals. The segment also revisits the concept of correlation and encourages reviewing materials for clarity.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And the estimated value is the projection of that point onto the line, and then you would see what that value is, right? So this is my ground truth why and this is why hat. I put hat as a representation of this is the predicted value or the estimated value. And you can see that based on this equation, the residual is a positive value for the points that are above the line. And you can also rationalize in the same way that for any point that's below the line, the residual is a negative value. You will see later on that there is a relationship between all of these residuals that are positive under residuals that are negative. And that's why the line generally passes through in the middle of the data point. So you have some positive residuals and some negative residuals. And we're going to see the... Mathematics magic behind why that's the case. So let's revisit correlation. So these are all reminders. So if you need to refresh your please go back and take a look at data aid or just calmly look at the slides after the lecture and it will all make sense. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

leaf_chunk.audio: And the estimated value is the projection of that point onto the line, and then you would see what that value is, right? So this is my ground truth why and this is why hat. I put hat as a representation of this is the predicted value or the estimated value. And you can see that based on this equation, the residual is a positive value for the points that are above the line. And you can also rationalize in the same way that for any point that's below the line, the residual is a negative value. You will see later on that there is a relationship between all of these residuals that are positive under residuals that are negative. And that's why the line generally passes through in the middle of the data point. So you have some positive residuals and some negative residuals. And we're going to see the... Mathematics magic behind why that's the case. So let's revisit correlation. So these are all reminders. So if you need to refresh your please go back and take a look at data aid or just calmly look at the slides after the lecture and it will all make sense.

children.title: Definition and Calculation of Correlation
children.summary: Correlation is defined as the average of the product of two variables, X and Y, both measured in standard units. Standard units imply that the data is zero-centered with a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. To standardize a dataset, one must calculate the average of the values, subtract the average from each individual sample, ensuring the mean is at zero. The formula for correlation is given as r = (1/n) * Σ[(xi - x̄)(yi - ȳ)], where xi and yi are the individual data points and x̄ and ȳ are their respective means.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What was correlation? So I want to break down the definition of correlation here for you. The definition from data aid textbook says that correlation is the average of the product of X and Y. When both are measured in a standard unit. Right? Three pieces of information. And let me tell you what they are. It's starting from measuring in a standard unit. When we say that a variable is an standard unit, that means that it's zero center to center deviation of one. Right? So the average of all the values should be zero. And then the standard deviation of all of the values should be equal to one. And the way that we actually standardize a data is to go through this process right here, that you take one column of values or one set of values. You calculate their average, you subtract the average from each individual sample. So that's how you will make sure that it's zero centered, meaning that the mean is at zero. children.title: Standardization and Pearson's Correlation Coefficient
children.summary: Standardization involves dividing transformations by the standard deviation of a data column to ensure a standard deviation of one. This allows for the measurement of variables X and Y in standard units. The average of the products of X and Y, both in standard units, is calculated by summing the products and dividing by n, leading to the definition of Pearson's correlation coefficient (r). The formula for r incorporates the covariance of X and Y, normalized by their respective standard deviations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then in order to make sure that the standard deviation is equal to one, you divide all of these transformations by standard deviation of that entire column or that set of values. This ensures that the standard deviation is equal to one as well. So that's what we mean by a standardization. So that's a standard unit. So when we say product of X and Y, both measured in a standard unit, this means that X measured in a standard unit is right here. Y measured in a standard unit is right here. So that's X and Y measured in a standard unit. And then there was an additional piece that says that the average of those products, and you're seeing that here I'm taking average by summing up all of these products and then dividing it by one over n, which is the average, right? So what is i, what is n? And let's go back to our data set. So I'm assuming that in this data set, I have a data frame with two columns. I'm going to call one column column x, one column column y, and then the rows are row one, row two, and then all the way to row n. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Calculating and Understanding Pearson's Correlation Coefficient
children.summary: Pearson's correlation coefficient is calculated using n data points represented as (xi, yi). The process involves zero centering or standardizing the x and y values, computing the product of these standardized values for each data point, summing these products, and then dividing by n to find the average. Correlation indicates a linear association between two variables, while covariance measures how they change together, with the relationship expressed as covariance equals correlation multiplied by the standard deviations of both variables. Correlation values range from -1 to 1, where a high correlation suggests suitability for simple linear regression, with positive correlation indicating that as x increases, y also increases.

children.title: Calculating Pearson's Correlation Coefficient
children.summary: To calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient, start with n data points in a dataset. Each data point is represented as (xi, yi). The first step is to zero center or standardize the x and y values. After standardization, compute the product of the standardized x and y for each data point, then sum these products. Finally, divide by n to find the average of these products. This correlation is also referred to as Pearson's coefficient. Additionally, covariance is defined as the average of the product of deviations from the mean for x and y.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So assume that I have n data points. So this data set d has n data points with row one b x1 y1, row two b x2 y2, and then all the way to x and yn. So what I need to do is go through each one of those rows, zero center or standardize all of those values. Once all of those values are standardized, I will basically try to calculate the product of the two x standard unit, y standard unit in each given row, and then I have a value. And then all of those values need to be summed up together and then divided by, or multiplied by 1 over n to calculate the average of those products that were measured, that were x and y measured instead of unit. That's what it is. It's a complicated kind of equation, but once you remember what it was, it will make sense. This correlation is also called Pearson's coefficient or Pearson's correlation coefficient as well. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Correlation and Covariance Definitions
children.summary: Correlation refers to a linear association between two variables, calculated using the formula that involves the average of the product of standardized values. Covariance, which measures how two variables change together, is derived from the means and standard deviations of the variables. The relationship between covariance and correlation is expressed by the equation: covariance equals correlation multiplied by the standard deviations of both variables. This segment also references previous content on correlation from Data 8, including the definition of Pearson's correlation coefficient.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So if you've heard multiple different terms for correlation, they all mean the correlation, and they all follow the same formula. If we don't want to standardize the data in a way that every column has a standard deviation of 1, But we want to make sure that there's zero centred, but they keep their standard deviation applying the similar formula is going to give us covariance. Covariance is basically meaning that remove the means, multiply them, and then pick the average. And this is the covariance. And covariance is equal to obviously based on the equation, is equal to correlation times the standard deviation of x times the standard deviation of y. These are repetitions from data aid just so that you know where that correlation was coming from when we were calculating this slope and kind of getting a sense of the fact that we needed to have correlation in order to figure out the relationship between X and X and Y. What we also know that is that the correlation shows us the linear association. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Regression Analysis
children.summary: Correlation measures the strength of a linear association between two variables, with values ranging from -1 to 1. A high correlation indicates a suitable case for applying simple linear regression. A correlation close to 1 signifies a perfect positive linear association, while a correlation of -1 indicates a perfect negative association. Positive correlation means that as variable x increases, variable y also increases. The segment also briefly defines Pearson's correlation coefficient and mentions covariance as a related concept.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It does not show us non-linear association. It does not show us causation in any sense. It just shows us the linear association or the degree of linear association between two variables x and y. And when the correlation is high enough, then in that case, it will make sense to just run simple linear regression or any regression analysis. Otherwise, if you don't see a linear relationship between two variables, why would you go and model it using a regression line? regression value is always between negative one and one. You can prove it because we standardize all of these all of these data points You can prove that Applying the equation is going to give you always a value between negative one and one When our or the correlation is close to one that's that shows a perfectly Positive linear association when the correlation is equal to negative one that's equal to perfect negative association. What is positive? What does negative mean? Positive means that by increasing x, y is also always increasing. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc

children.title: Correlation and Linear Association Explained
children.summary: Positive slope indicates a positive correlation, while a negative correlation shows that as x increases, y decreases, resulting in a negative slope. A correlation of zero signifies a weak linear association, indicating no influence between the variables, though it does not imply no association at all. Linear association does not equate to causation. Pearson's correlation coefficient measures the strength of linear association between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1, with covariance defined as the average of the product of deviations from the mean.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's a positive slope, right? Negative correlation means that by negative is strong correlation means that by increasing x, your y is going to decrease. So that's a negative slope and that's why correlation that you have is going to control whether you're going to get a positive slope or a negative slope in your dataset or in your linear regression. When correlation is equal to zero, it shows that we have a weak linear association. It doesn't tell us that there is no association between them. It just says that the linear association is weak if you're concerned with linear association. And obviously I said that linear association does not mean causation. In any sense, it just means linear association for our purposes. When R is equal to zero, we would also say that the two variables are uncorrelated, meaning that there is no correlation between them. There is no reason that one increasing or decreasing one is going to have any influence or is going to show any influence on the other one. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Correlation and Linear Association Explained
children.summary: Correlation is defined as a value between -1 and 1, indicating the strength of a linear association between two variables. A correlation value of 0 suggests no correlation, while values close to 1 or -1 indicate strong positive or negative associations, respectively. Examples illustrate that random distributions yield small correlation values, significant positive correlations are close to 1, and negative correlations show decreasing trends in y as x increases. It is important to note that correlation does not imply causation or account for nonlinear relationships.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So there is no correlation between them. We would say that they're uncorrelated. correlation is a value between negative 1 and 1. So that's what I'm showing that the absolute value of r is always less than 1. And here are some examples. For example, in the first case, you're seeing that there is absolutely no clear sign that there is correlation or linear association between x and y. Right. So take the x axis always as x and then y axis as a second variable. it looks like that is randomly distributed. So that's why you're seeing that the correlation is also a very small value. Here in this case, we're seeing a significant positive correlation or a linear association between x and y. That's why it's also shown by this value that's very close to 1. In the third case, what you're seeing is that as we're increasing x, the y decreases, right? Roughly y decreases. There's a little bit of noise obviously, but what we're expecting is that we're expecting to have some linear association, but the linear association is negative. Video: 1: lec10.ipynb - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: So there is no correlation between them. We would say that they're uncorrelated. correlation is a value between negative 1 and 1. So that's what I'm showing that the absolute value of r is always less than 1. And here are some examples. For example, in the first case, you're seeing that there is absolutely no clear sign that there is correlation or linear association between x and y. Right. So take the x axis always as x and then y axis as a second variable. it looks like that is randomly distributed. So that's why you're seeing that the correlation is also a very small value. Here in this case, we're seeing a significant positive correlation or a linear association between x and y. That's why it's also shown by this value that's very close to 1. In the third case, what you're seeing is that as we're increasing x, the y decreases, right? Roughly y decreases. There's a little bit of noise obviously, but what we're expecting is that we're expecting to have some linear association, but the linear association is negative.

children.title: Correlation, Regression Line Derivation, and Models
children.summary: The segment covers correlation concepts, illustrating a negative correlation of -0.7 versus a stronger correlation of 0.9, and discusses how data noise affects linear relationships. Pearson's correlation coefficient is defined as a measure of linear association strength. In simple linear regression, a zero-centered dataset leads to a regression line through the origin, with positive and negative correlations indicating trends. The importance of interpreting results in original data units is emphasized, and the regression line is adjusted to pass through averages of x and y. The derivation of the regression line equation is explained, starting with y hat, incorporating the correlation coefficient and standard deviations. The slope (b hat) is defined as the product of the correlation coefficient and standard deviations' ratio, with the intercept calculated as y bar minus the slope times x bar. Residuals are defined as the difference between actual and predicted values, and the modeling process highlights the significance of datasets in regression analysis.

children.title: Correlation and Linear Regression Concepts
children.summary: The segment covers the concept of correlation, illustrating a negative correlation of -0.7, which indicates a weaker linear association compared to a correlation of 0.9. It discusses how data noise affects the linear relationship between variables x and y and introduces the idea of a quadratic relationship, where correlation would be close to zero. Pearson's correlation coefficient is defined as a measure of the strength of linear association. In the context of simple linear regression, it is explained that a zero-centered dataset results in a regression line passing through the origin, with positive and negative correlations indicating increasing and decreasing trends, respectively. The importance of interpreting results in original data units is emphasized, and the regression line is adjusted to pass through the averages of x and y, reflecting the relationship between standard deviations of the variables. The equation for the regression line in original units is discussed, incorporating averages and standard deviations.

children.title: Correlation and Linear Association Concepts
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of correlation, highlighting a specific case where the correlation is negative 0.7, indicating a weaker linear association compared to a previous case with a correlation of 0.9. It emphasizes that while there is some linear relationship between variables x and y, the presence of data noise affects this association. Additionally, it introduces the idea of a quadratic relationship, noting that if the correlation is calculated for such a relationship, it would be close to zero due to the lack of a linear pattern. The segment also defines Pearson's correlation coefficient and explains that correlation measures the strength of a linear association between two variables.

Audio: And the valid digesting here is negative 0.7. And comparing negative points 7 with point 9.5 that we had in the second case, you were seeing that the linear association is this slightly weaker because we have some spread around some spread in the data or some noise in the data that is kind of keeping us from having a perfect linear association. But despite that, that issue we still see some linear association. In the last case, what would you guess the correlation be? The correlation, so this is kind of where I'm going to try to re-emphasize that what we're seeing in this case is that there is some relationship between x and y, right? What we're seeing in fact is a quadratic relationship. But since it's not a linear relationship, if you go ahead and calculate correlation, it's going to give us a correlation of close to zero. children.title: Understanding Correlation in Linear Regression
children.summary: Correlation in simple linear regression is discussed, emphasizing that when data is zero-centered, the regression line passes through the origin. A positive correlation (R) indicates an increasing trend, while a negative R indicates a decreasing trend. The importance of interpreting results in the original units of the data rather than standard units is highlighted. Additionally, Pearson's correlation coefficient is defined as the average of the product of x and y measured in standard units, with covariance also mentioned as a related concept.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But there is non-linear pattern that can be captured using other methods or other kinds of modeling that we can do. So moving on and kind of talking about what does it mean when we calculate correlation? When we have correlation of R in a simple linear regression data, simple linear regression word, and when our data is zero centered, meaning that when our data is zero centered, our regression line is going to pass through the origin. And then we talk about the correlation. That means that by every one unit increase in X, the Y value is going to increase by our unit. And again, notice that if R is positive, that means that it's an increasing trend, when R is negative, that means that that's a decreasing trend, that's a negative slope, and that's how you can make sense of what's happening in the regression kind of line. But we don't want to live in the standard unit word. What we want to live in is in the original unit or the original kind of composition of the data set that we had. children.title: Understanding Regression Lines in Original Units
children.summary: The regression line is adjusted to pass through the averages of x and y rather than the origin, as data points are moved back into their original units. This adjustment reflects the relationship between standard deviations of x and y, where for each standard deviation unit of x, y changes by r times the standard deviation of y. The equation for the regression line in original units incorporates the averages and standard deviations of both variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what we're going to do is we're going to move back all of our data all of our data sets or data points into the stand into the original units that they were meaning that our data point or our. A regression line is going to pass through average of x and average of y instead of passing through the origin. Right. Again, because we removed the, removed average of x from all of the x values and removed average of y from all the y values. When we added back, then it will basically push all of the data points by a factor of average of x and average of y. And that's why the origin is going to move to the point that corresponds to average of x and average of y. What you're seeing here is that x is the scale appropriately. Here we would say that for every standard deviation unit of x, y is going to be changed by r times the standard deviation of y. Right? That's how, again, you're scaling across the x, a scaling across the y because previously you divided by standard deviation of x and a standard deviation of y. children.title: Regression Line in Standard and Original Units
children.summary: The regression line for predicting y based on x in standard units has a slope of r and passes through the origin, represented by the equation y = r * x. When converting to original units, the equation becomes y - average of y = r * (x - average of x) * (SD of y / SD of x). This segment discusses how to transition from standard units to original units while maintaining the relationship between the variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If you multiply that back, this is what you're going to get. One is going to be multiplied by standard deviation of x, and R is going to be multiplied by standard deviation of y. That's why this scale is going to be different. So the equation that we will have, and this is the equation that you had seen in data 8 is that what we have is the equation for this line is y hat or the prediction for y minus y bar divided by a standard deviation of y. This is kind of moving all of these, all of the points or all of the equations from the top equation down to the bottom equation, but moving it to non-standard unit. So when I had y hat before, I'm going to here say that this y hat was measured in a standard unit, but the non-standard unit is going to be this y hat. So y hat minus average of y divided by standard division of x, I'm moving r down and then I'm also saying that the way that I calculated x in the standard unit was that x minus x bar divided by standard division of x. children.title: Simplifying the Regression Equation
children.summary: The focus is on simplifying the regression equation to isolate y hat on one side and the remaining terms on the other. The goal is to identify the coefficient (intercept) and slope (be hat) of the equation. To simplify, both sides are multiplied by the standard deviation of y, leading to the equation y hat minus y bar equals r times the standard deviation of y multiplied by x. The regression line in standard units is discussed, noting that it passes through the origin with the equation y = r * x. In original units, the equation is expressed as y - y bar equals r times x, incorporating the standard deviations of both y and x.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's the equation that we'll be working with. If I move this equation to this address slide and try to simplify and just make sure that why hat remains on one side and then everything else is going to be remained on the right hand side and then I'm basically trying to get the coefficient, get a hat which is your intercept and also be hat which was your slope then we can basically figure out what is the the interest of equation and what is the, what is this little equation in our case. So let me start doing that. From this point and on, I might write equations on this slide, but when I publish this slide, the slides are going to have animations so you can actually walk through the animation and figure out all the steps. So what I'm going to do in order to simplify this equation is that I'm going to multiply both sides by standard deviation of y so that I can get the rate of the denominator in the left hand side. So what I'll have is y hat minus y bar is equal to r times the standard deviation of y multiplied by. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Deriving Regression Line Equation and Components
children.summary: The segment covers the derivation of the regression line equation, starting with the formula for y hat, which includes the correlation coefficient and standard deviations of x and y. It explains how to isolate the coefficient for x (B hat) and presents the final regression line equation. The slope (b hat) is defined as the product of the correlation coefficient R and the ratio of standard deviations, illustrating the relationship between changes in X and Y. The intercept is calculated as y bar minus the slope times x bar, and residuals are defined as the difference between actual and predicted values. The modeling process is also discussed, emphasizing the importance of datasets in regression analysis.

children.title: Deriving the Regression Line Equation
children.summary: The lecture segment discusses the derivation of the regression line equation. It begins with the formula for y hat, which is expressed as r times the standard deviation of y divided by the standard deviation of x, multiplied by (x minus x bar), plus y bar. The goal is to isolate the coefficient for x, denoted as B hat, by rearranging the equation. The final form of the regression line is presented as y hat equal to the product of the correlation coefficient and the ratio of standard deviations, multiplied by x, plus a constant term.

children.title: Slope Calculation in Regression Analysis
children.summary: The slope of the regression line, denoted as b hat, is expressed as the product of the correlation coefficient R and the ratio of the standard deviations of Y and X. This relationship shows that for each standard unit increase in X, Y increases by R times the standard deviation of Y. The regression line equation is recalled as y = â + bx, emphasizing the connection between correlation, standard deviation, and the slope.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then the other thing that I'll have is Y bar. Minus are times the standard deviation of y divided by standard deviation of x times x bar. Right? I just rearranged some of the turns. So if we go ahead and take a look at what we had, the coefficient that's multiplied by x is going to be my slope or is going to be my b hat, which that coefficient right here is exactly what we're seeing right here. So this slope is going to be, I'm going to write it here, that this slope is going to be equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. This is what we had seen before. We just gave it to you in data 8 that this slope is equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. of x but this time we actually drive it for you. Brand the fact that What was correlation, what was the standard unit, and then how did we, what is the actual shape of the line that we have with every one standard unit, kind of increment in X, Y is going to be increased by our times the standard deviation of Y, and then technically that's how we arrived at this equation. children.title: Intercept and Residuals in Regression
children.summary: The intercept in the regression equation is expressed as y bar minus the slope times x bar. The slope is defined as y bar minus r times y bar minus the slope times x bar. The residual, or error in prediction for data point i, is calculated as the exact value minus the predicted value (y hat). This segment emphasizes the derivation of both the slope and intercept, leading to the overall equation of the regression line.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Then the only other thing that I need to talk about is the intercept. Everything else, everything else that's remaining here is going to be intercept. I'm going to write that out that this is y bar minus. What you're noticing here is that this term right here is exactly what I had for this slope. So I can directly say that that term is equal to slope. So I have y bar minus slope. Times. Explore. And that was the equation for the slope that we had from before. That the slope is always y bar minus r times y bar minus a slope times x bar. And then obviously, what we have for the residual is that y residual or the error in prediction for data point i is equal to the exact value minus the predicted value y i hat. hat and I use hat because that's a predicted value and that's not the exact value that we calculate. And then why I had you can actually derive it using this equation which now we derive both this lobe and interest of it we can directly have the equation of the line and actually go ahead and calculate it. children.title: Modeling Process and Regression Line Derivation
children.summary: The lecture outlines the modeling process, emphasizing that every modeling task begins with a dataset containing one or more attributes (inputs) and a target variable (Y). Each observation in the dataset corresponds to a row, which includes features for modeling, particularly in simple linear regression. The goal is to derive and define the regression line equation, including components such as the slope and intercept, as well as the error for each data point.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the goal that I have for today's lecture is actually go ahead and derive all of these equations one more time using calculus and then we'll see how we can actually do that. And what's the formal way of us defining how good of a model, how good a model can be and how we can objectively evaluate it. So let's go through the modeling process. So this is the process that we will go over every time that we want to build a model. Up to the end of the data 100 lecture, every time that I want to talk about creating a new model, I'm going to follow this same modeling process. And let me tell you what this modeling process involves. The first step of the modeling process is that every time that we start doing a modeling, there is going to be a data set. Your data set is going to have one or more attributes or inputs that we're predicting, and then it's always going to have a Y or a target variable that we're trying to match it to. So there. Each row of our data frame that we're providing you is going to be an observation, which one piece of that is going to have inputs, features or attributes that we're going to model in the simple linear regression board. children.title: Simple vs. Multiple Linear Regression Concepts
children.summary: In linear regression, one input variable (X) is used to predict a single output variable (Y). Simple linear regression focuses on one feature for prediction, while multiple regression involves several features combined to predict the target variable. Predictions are denoted as y hats (ŷ), with each observation having its own predicted value, such as ŷ1, ŷ2, ..., ŷn.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X
lec10.ipynb - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat.

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression and Parametric Models
children.summary: This segment introduces simple linear regression models, emphasizing their equation which includes an intercept and a slope multiplied by the input variable. It explains that all models will be parametric, meaning parameters (TETA values) are learned from data for accurate estimations. Future topics will include constant models and multiple linear regression. Parametric models are defined by specific parameters, with the goal of determining optimal TETA values (TETA hats) to model datasets effectively. The relationship between independent and dependent variables is described through mathematical functions, with the model equation expressed as y hat = theta 0 + theta 1 times x. The segment clarifies that while x and y values are observed, parameters are informed by data, and distinctions are made between estimates and predictions.

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression Models
children.summary: The segment introduces simple linear regression, highlighting its equation that includes an intercept term and a slope multiplied by the input variable. It mentions that all models discussed will be parametric, meaning parameters (theta values) will be learned from data to enable accurate estimations. Future lectures will cover constant models and multiple linear regression, expanding on the concept of models as mathematical functions to describe relationships between independent and dependent variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In terms of the models that we will build today and also in the next couple of lectures, we're going to start by building a simple linear regression. So, a simple linear regression is going as having equation like this, that there's always an intercept term, tetasera, and then there's always the slope that we're always referring to that as teta1 times input, and then we will combine the two terms in order to get the production. We will also see other types of models in terms of lecture and also next Tuesday that is going to be constant model and multiple linear regression that we'll talk about them later. I want to put out there that all the models that we'll be discussing, they're called parametric models. Because using the data, we will learn parameters of the model, all of those theta values are parameters that once we know those parameters and once we know what kind of model we use, then we can directly do the estimation of each one, or the calculation of each one of the estimated values as accurately as possible. children.title: Parametric Models and TETA Parameters
children.summary: Parametric models are defined by specific parameters, referred to as TETA values, which are the only parameters in the modeling process. The x and y values represent inputs and outputs, not parameters. The optimum set of TETA values, denoted as TETA hats, is determined to accurately model the dataset. A model is essentially a mathematical function that describes the relationships between variables. The segment also introduces the concept of using models to predict outcomes based on independent and dependent variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So these are the parameters that we use for the modeling purposes and that's why there are parametric models. When we say that we have all of these parametric models, the only parameters that we have are all of those slow-band interest step and those coefficient values. Everything else is going to be input or the features that we're using. So in other words, the x and y values are not our parameters. So only the TETA values are our parameters. When I say that I use a parametric model that is parametrized by the TETA value? Then I will go ahead and drive what is the optimum set of TETA values that is going to be as accurately as possible model through my entire data set. We call them TETA hats. Hat on top of TETA means that there was a model that now this TETA parameter for that model is the optimum set of parameters. And I'm going to show you what that means if a model is not optimum, because technically when you define a model parameter, or when you're defining a parametric model, you're just defining a function or a mathematical function. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Understanding Parametric Models and Optimal Parameters
children.summary: Parametric models are characterized by a limited number of parameters, such as theta 0 and theta 1. The goal is not to find a single optimum mathematical function for the entire dataset but to define a family of functions that can model the relationship within the data. The model equation is expressed as y hat = theta 0 + theta 1 times x. The optimal parameter values, denoted with a hat, provide the best predictions for the dataset. It's important to note that while x and y values are observed directly, the parameters are informed by the data. The estimate of a parameter is represented as ê, distinguishing between estimate and prediction. Typically, parameters are selected based on a chosen criterion for optimality.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You're not trying to find out what is the optimum mathematical function that models your entire dataset. So this is what it means. When we have a model, we're basically trying to have, could define the family of functions that can be used in order to model our relationship. We would say that, okay, if I have the model parameters to be theta, then my model equation is going to be y hat equal to teta 0 plus teta 1 times x. When I say that I have the best optimum teta value, I will go ahead and put a hat on top of it, which basically means that this time, if you go ahead and calculate y hat values using these optimum values, this is the best prediction that you can have an average on the entire data set that we have. Again, this right here is going to be a family of the models that can be applied to our data set, but there is only one set of values that is tetA0 hadn't tetA1 had that are going to be optimal. children.title: Transition to Theta Parameters in Linear Regression
children.summary: The lecture discusses the shift from using A and B to theta parameters (θ0, θ1) in linear regression models in Data 100. This change is significant as it allows for the inclusion of multiple features or attributes in a model, each associated with its own coefficient represented as a theta value. By combining these coefficients into a vector, the model can efficiently utilize parameters, facilitating the understanding of the model being used. The segment also emphasizes that while x and y values are observed data, the parameters are informed by the data, and the estimated parameters (denoted as ê) represent the best fit according to selected criteria.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, and that's that that's where what we're going to be after. In data 100, one thing that you notice is that we're moving away from using A and B for linear regression and we're moving into the TETA 0 and TETA 1 ward. And that's very important. We're not using TETA 0 and TETA 1 because we think we're fancy, but this is because what I can do is that if I keep adding features or attribute to my model, then what I can have is that I can have a coefficient or the theta value associated with each one of those attributes, and I can combine all of them into a vector. So for example, in this case, what I've done is that I'm saying that my model uses theta zero and theta one. Let's combine them into a vector of theta. And you pass me theta or you pass me theta hat. I know what model you're using. I know what parameters you're using, and I can go ahead and use those parameters, right? Instead of just go to a, b, c, d, and so on and so forth. this way just using the subscript of i, tetasero, tetah1, tetah2, and so on and so forth. Video: 1: lec10.ipynb - JupyterLab

children.title: Parametric vs Non-parametric Models Overview
children.summary: Parametric models are defined by a limited number of parameters, which are informed by the data rather than being predetermined. In contrast, non-parametric models, such as Kernel Density Estimates (KDE) and K-nearest neighbors, do not require parameters to be set. The focus of the course will be on learning and deriving the parameters for parametric models to create unique models. The segment also distinguishes between observed values (x, y) and estimated parameters, noting that the 'hat' notation indicates estimates versus predictions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we can do is we can consolidate them into a tetavector and then we can index into the relationship between each feature has what weight more accurately or more easily. So that's kind of the notation that we'll be using. I said here we have a parametric model. We have seen non parametric models before. So some statistical models do not have parameters. For example, we have seen KDE's. KDE's were models, or kernel density estimate. But we did not set any parameters. We would just estimate alpha, and then we would just run a process, and we would have KDE's. Kairi's neighbors is also another model that you did not need to set parameters, or there was not no parameter to learn. So those are the cases that there are models that are non-parametric, there are models that are parametric, but we're dealing with is parametric models. And what we're going to do throughout this multiple lecture is to learn those parameters, how to optimally learn those parameters, how to derive those parameters so that we can uniquely have our own model to work with. children.title: Modeling Process and Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The modeling process includes selecting a model category, choosing a loss function to quantify prediction error, and optimizing this function to find the best parameters. The discussion transitions to model evaluation, specifically highlighting the optimization of parameters in simple linear regression (SLR) and the introduction of new notation, such as TETA and TETA hats. Key parameters include the slope and intercept, with 'x' as the input variable. The segment emphasizes defining a loss function to assess model prediction accuracy and the significance of optimizing the cost function to achieve optimal parameters.

children.title: Steps in the Modeling Process
children.summary: The modeling process involves several key steps: first, selecting the category of model to use, such as simple linear regression; second, choosing a loss function that quantifies prediction error; and finally, optimizing the loss function to determine the best parameters for the model. Further details on loss functions and optimization will be provided in upcoming slides.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the terminology and we know the notation that we'll be using, let me walk you through the modeling process. Every time that we want to build a model, irrespective of what model you're building, you first need to choose the category of model that you want to use. So for example, for today's lecture, what we would say is that our model is going to be a simple linear regression. We might change our mind in the next few lectures, and we will keep using different types of models. But the first step is that you figure out what framework you want to use. The second step is to choose a loss function. What is a loss function? Loss function is a function that will tell you how good your bad your predictions are. And I'm going to objectively define what this loss function means and how to choose a loss function in the next few slides. Once we have the model and we have the loss function, we'll go through an optimization process. This is a fun part that we'll actually go ahead and try to optimize our loss function in a way that we find the best set of parameters for our model. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Model Evaluation and Linear Regression Basics
children.summary: The segment discusses the transition from TETA to TETA hats, indicating the optimization of the model and the subsequent need for evaluation criteria to ensure the model's performance on the dataset. It introduces simple linear regression as the chosen model for the lecture, emphasizing the new notation and terminology. The discussion highlights the TETA vector as a means to generate predictions based on input values, setting the stage for understanding the modeling process, which includes choosing a model, selecting a loss function to quantify prediction error, and fitting the model to determine the best parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we go from having TETA up to having TETA hats. And once we have TETA hats, we have our model, we have optimized model, and we're ready to go. Once we have our TETA hat and we fit the model and we find the optimum parameters, we need to evaluate the model. So we're going to briefly talk about whether the evaluation criteria that we'll have after we have the model to make sure that our model is performing well on the data set that we have. So let's start with choosing a model, which should be simple in this case. We're choosing simple linear regression for the purpose of today's lecture, because today's lecture is just basically setting you up with all the new notation and the new terminology that we're using. We're going from AMB, we're going into the word of TETA, so we're using TETA 0 as my Y interest of and TETA 1 as this loop that we're using. So what I said was that this tetah vector that we have is the finding a family of functions. So what that means is that if you are saying that you have a tetah vector and you pass the values of tetasurant, tetah one of that tetah vector, you pass the input, you have the predictions. children.title: Understanding Simple Linear Regression Parameters
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of finding optimal parameters in a simple linear regression (SLR) model to achieve the best estimations. It explains the notation used, specifically the 'hat' symbol indicating estimations and predictions. The parameters for the model, represented as a vector, include the slope and intercept, while 'x' is identified as the input rather than a parameter. The segment concludes with an affirmation that the first step in the modeling process has been completed.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So think about that as a machine that throughout the modeling process will try to find the parameters for this machine that gives us the best estimations, right? So that's what it means. What we have is that the parameters are sitting in the middle and we're trying to find the optimums that are parameters in the middle to get the best estimations, the best estimations as much as possible. So one thing to notice here is that I'm using also hat on top of the estimations. And I was using hat one more time when I was putting it on top of tetas. This is kind of operate or overloading that I'm using. So when it happens on top of why that means that's an estimation. When hat is on top of, or that's the prediction. When hat is on top of the teta, that means that that's the optimum set of parameters for the model that I'm using. The first step is done. We have a linear, simple linear regression. children.title: Defining Loss Function and Model Evaluation
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of defining a loss function to evaluate how well a model predicts target values. It introduces the concept of calculating predictions using parameters theta 0 and theta 1, leading to the prediction of y (y hat). The importance of optimizing the cost function to find the optimal parameters (theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat) is emphasized. Additionally, the modeling process is outlined, which includes choosing a model, selecting a loss function to quantify prediction error, fitting the model to data, and evaluating model performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We know that the pattern of the function is going to be that two parameters are going to be given, and then we can calculate the prediction of y, which is y hat, using teta 0 plus theta 1 times x. Now we need to get into what is the loss function? And how can we define how good we are matching to the target values based on different models that we have? So we are going to actually follow a certain process that defining a cost function and optimizing the cost function up to the point that we get to the optimum set of parameters that I'm referring to as theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. So choosing a loss function, how can we quantify how good or bad a function is map to our data set? So for example, what you're seeing here is that I have tree lines. Again, remember that that machine in the middle was defining a family of lines. So here in this case, I'm just showing out tree random kind of sets of values for the the tetah vector.
