children.title: Evaluating Model Accuracy with Data Visualization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on assessing which of three linear models best fits a given data set by analyzing the shape of the lines and the distribution of the data. The blue line is identified as the most accurate representation of the data. Participants are prompted to consider how they define accuracy in this context and to think about the 'optimal parameters' for the linear model, using the example of estimating the number of characters in each chapter of 'Little Women' based on the number of periods.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So here, tetah 0, tetah 1, tetah 0, tetah 1 for three separate lines. And then I draw all of those lines. So what we're wondering is that just looking at the shape of these lines and the distribution of the data, which one of these models are more accurate. And then think about how you're going to measure in your mind what accuracy means. This is your first slide of the day, which one of these lines matches the data set more accurately. OK, it's kind of clear that the blue line is matching our data said more accurately. children.title: Understanding Loss Functions and Their Impact
children.summary: This segment explains loss functions' role in evaluating prediction accuracy by comparing predicted values (y hat) to actual values (y), emphasizing that smaller loss indicates better model performance. It distinguishes between L1 (absolute loss) and L2 (squared loss), discussing their implications for model accuracy, computational efficiency, and handling of outliers. The choice between L1 and L2 is analyzed, noting that squared loss penalizes larger residuals more heavily, which can distort predictions in the presence of outliers. The calculation of average loss is described, where the loss for each data point is summed and divided by the total number of points, ensuring positive cost values for fair comparisons. The segment also introduces empirical risk, highlighting how changes in the TETA value affect the model and predictions, with a focus on the significance of L2 and L1 loss in model performance.

children.title: Understanding and Types of Loss Functions
children.summary: The segment explains loss functions in modeling, emphasizing their role in evaluating prediction accuracy by comparing predicted values (y hat) to actual values (y). It highlights that a smaller loss indicates better model performance, with the blue line representing minimum average distance from data points compared to the higher error of the red line. The importance of loss functions varies by task, particularly in classification versus regression. It details L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss) as common measures, discussing their impact on model accuracy, computational efficiency, and considerations for outliers and error costs.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Modeling
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of loss functions in modeling, explaining how to evaluate the accuracy of predictions. It highlights that the blue line represents the minimum average distance from data points compared to the red line, which accumulates high error. The loss function quantifies the accuracy of predictions by comparing predicted values (y hat) to actual values (y). A smaller loss indicates closer predictions to actual values, reflecting better model performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm looking at how far the points are from each one of those lines. And it looks like that when I'm looking at the distances from the line, the blue line has the minimum distance on average across all of the across all of the points in a data set versus, for example, if you're looking at the red line, if you want to calculate the distances of all of the points from the line, that's a very high error that we're accumulating throughout the entire data set. So we're defining the loss function in the same way. We would say that we're going to take a look at the prediction predicted values. We're going to take a look at the real values. And we're going to have a single up to single value that assigned to how bad the predictions are. So what we would say is that if y hat is close to y, we would give you a little loss. law. So we would say that you did not do that badly and then we would give a value in our, which is a small value. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Importance and Types of Loss Functions
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of loss functions in evaluating the accuracy of predictions in modeling. A loss function quantifies the error or fit of a model based on how far predictions (Y hat) are from actual values (Y). It penalizes models more for larger discrepancies and less for closer predictions. The choice of loss function varies depending on the task, such as classification versus regression, and will be explored in detail in the course.

leaf_chunk.text:

children.title: Loss Functions in Classification Tasks
children.summary: In classification tasks, such as cancer identification, it is crucial to select appropriate loss functions to avoid missing potential cases. Two common loss functions are L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss), which measure the difference between actual and predicted values. L2 loss calculates the squared residuals, while L1 loss focuses on the absolute differences. The choice of loss function impacts model accuracy and computational efficiency, and it varies based on the task, including considerations for outliers and the cost of different types of errors.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If you're doing a classification task, for example, if you're doing cancer identification in a sense, you really don't want to miss any potential cancer patients. So in those cases, you really want to have a different kinds of loss function that accounts for all of those cases as well. So we're going to take a look at all of those considerations when we go into evaluating the models a little bit more, or just putting it out there that when I talk about loss function today, I'm going to introduce two loss functions, but those are not the only loss functions that we use. Depending on the use case, the loss function can be different. So, last function of the day, you might have seen these equations before. One last function is called L2 loss or a squirt loss. One loss is called absolute loss or L1 loss, order 1, order 2. Right? Think about them like that. So the squirt loss basically says that go ahead and take a look at the actual value, subtract the predicted value, so meaning that calculate the residual, and go ahead and score the residual. children.title: L1 and L2 Loss Functions Explained
children.summary: The segment discusses L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss) in the context of scoring errors in predictions. L2 loss penalizes larger residuals more heavily by squaring the error, leading to a significant penalty for larger discrepancies, while L1 loss treats residuals linearly, applying no additional penalty beyond the absolute value of the error. Both loss functions aim to assess the quality of predictions, with L2 loss providing a stronger penalty for larger errors compared to L1 loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So anytime that you saw an error, a score that error, which basically means that if your error is very a small value, I'm scoring it and I'm even making it smaller, right? So if you're taking a look at the residual being a value between zero and one and you score it, that's even going to get a smaller. So it's not going to be penalized as much. Compared to the case that, okay, If you have 100 units of residual, it will square it to penalize you even more. So the penalty right now is going to be 10,000 in that case. So that's kind of L2 loss or the second order loss that we have. The absolute loss just looks at the residual and calculus the absolute value. Again, meaning that if it's the smallest, it's just going to be that same absolute value of the residual. If it's 100 unit of residual, it's just going to be same 100 units. It's not going to be penalized any further. But in both of the cases, they're penalizing. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: L1 and L2 Loss in Linear Regression
children.summary: The loss in linear regression is always positive, as it represents a cost. In the context of simple linear regression, the loss is defined using L2 (squared loss) and L1 (absolute loss) functions. For L2 loss, it is expressed as the square of the difference between the actual value (y) and the predicted value (y hat), while L1 loss is the absolute difference. Both loss functions help evaluate how closely a line fits all data points in a dataset, with a good fit resulting in minimal loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And in both of the cases, what you notice is that the loss is always a positive value because it's a cost. So we want to give a cost. We don't want to give a negative cost. So in the cost in both of the cases, in our case, is positive. So what does it mean in the... in the language of linear regression, that means that I'm going to go ahead and replace in every instance that I have y hat, I'm going to replace it with the equation for the simple linear regression. So here I have y minus y hat, so here my loss for simple linear regression based on L2 or a squared loss is going to be y minus this right here is y hat and then the squared. Same thing for the L1 loss, we just plug in y hat and then we have the equation. Note to step what I have right here is the loss or the cost or a single data point. But what we want is we want to have a line that matches all of the data sets, all of the data points in the data set as closely as possible. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Comparison of L1 and L2 Loss Functions and Their Impact
children.summary: The segment discusses the choice between L1 (absolute loss) and L2 (squared loss) in predictive modeling, highlighting that squared loss penalizes larger residuals more heavily, which can skew model predictions in the presence of outliers. It raises concerns about the influence of outliers on data trends and the rationale behind not using residual values directly, emphasizing the importance of using absolute or squared loss to avoid cancellation of positive and negative residuals. The calculation of average loss is explained, where the loss for each data point is summed and divided by the total number of points to ensure positive cost values, facilitating fair model performance comparisons. Additionally, the role of parametric functions and empirical risk in model training is introduced.

children.title: Comparison of L1 and L2 Loss Functions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the choice between absolute loss (L1) and squared loss (L2) in penalizing residuals in predictive modeling. It emphasizes that squared loss is more ideal for penalizing large residuals more than small ones, as it disproportionately increases the penalty for larger miscalculations. A drawback of this approach is mentioned, though not elaborated.

children.title: Impact of Outliers on Loss Functions
children.summary: Outliers can significantly affect the trend of data, leading models to adjust their predictions to minimize loss. When large residuals are present, models may tilt their predictions to reduce the squared loss incurred by outliers, potentially deviating from the actual data trend. The discussion also raises the question of why residual values are not used directly, prompting a poll on the ideal loss function for penalizing large residuals more than small ones, with options including squared loss and absolute loss.

And the model, if model wants to try to avoid having that squared large loss that it might incur, it might tilt the line or the model from the actual trend that it needs to be to the trend that's kind of have meets halfway in between. So that it gets some small loss on the actual trend that the data and also gets closer to the outlier data point that it has. What about this question? children.title: Importance of Absolute and Squared Loss
children.summary: The discussion focuses on why residual errors are not used directly in calculations, emphasizing that using absolute loss or squared loss helps avoid issues with positive and negative residuals canceling each other out. It highlights the necessity of ensuring all cost values are positive to prevent the sum from equaling zero, which is crucial for accurate data aggregation and analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why do we score them? Why do we calculate absolute value? Why do we want all of them to be all of the cost values to be positive? Why do we want to get rid of negatives? Sum will be zero. Residual sum to zero, that is correct. Um... Okay, they don't cancel out. So that seems to be the right trend. But the issue that we have when we try to just use the actual residual values is that some residual values are positive, some residual values are negative, right? This is what we saw before. And if we just actually get the residual values and sum them up, the issue is going to be there, going to cancel each other out. And again, we need to calculate the cost and aggregate in the entire data sets. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Calculating Average Loss for Model Training
children.summary: To ensure all cost values are positive, absolute loss or squared loss is used. The loss for each data point is calculated and summed, then divided by the total number of data points (n) to obtain the average loss. This method allows for fair comparison of model performance across different datasets without being influenced by dataset size. The objective function used in training the model aims to find the optimal set of parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's why we really want to make sure that all of them are positive cost values. And that's why we're using either absolute loss or a squared loss in all of these cases. I said we need to make sure that we calculate the loss for all of the data points. In fact, the way that we do it is that we will go ahead and sum up all of these loss values for all of the data points. So you see why I, why I have. That means that the prediction for data point I and assuming that we have n different data points, we will sum up all of those loss values that we have and then we will divide it by n, which is going to be calculating the average. Right? Summing up makes sense because I want to take a look at the entire data set. When I'm dividing it by n, this is the case that if I'm comparing the model performances on different data sets, I don't want the sides of the data sets to be influencing my decision about which model is performing better, right? Or which model is performing? We'll object the function that we'll be using in order to train a model and find the optimum set of parameters that we have. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Parametric Functions and Empirical Risk
children.summary: The function discussed is a parametric function dependent on theta, where different theta values yield different predictions (y-hats) and consequently different losses for data points. The objective is to find the theta values that minimize the overall cost function, which represents the average loss across all data points. This average loss, known as empirical risk, measures the model's prediction accuracy for the entire dataset, not just individual points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One note is that what you notice is that this function is a parametric function. It's parametrized by theta, meaning that if you give me different theta values here, I'm going to give you different y-hats. And by having different y-hats, you're going to have different losses for the data points. And then once you have different losses, you're going to have a different overall cost or the overall average loss. So, kind of notice that this function, depending on the teta is going to change, and our goal in this case is going to be to find the teta value or the set of teta zero and teta one values that minimizes this cost function that goes through all of the data points as closely as possible on average and have the minimum average residual values that we have. Okay, does that make sense? So this cost function is a really important cost function. Again, it's parametrized by TETA, meaning that depending on the TETA value, the value of the cost on the data set is going to change. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling X
lec10.ipynb - JupyterLab

children.title: Empirical Risk, Loss Functions, and Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment covers empirical risk and loss functions in modeling, highlighting how changes in the TETA value affect the model, cost function, and predictions. It discusses empirical risk as the average loss using L2 loss (mean squared error) and L1 loss (mean absolute error), emphasizing the significance of these functions in model performance. The discussion on L2 loss details its calculation through residual squares in least squares regression, aiming to minimize errors across the dataset. Fitting simple linear regression models involves optimizing parameters theta 0 and theta 1 by minimizing the cost function, with a focus on calculating partial derivatives of the mean squared error. The overall modeling process includes model selection, loss function choice, fitting, and performance evaluation.

children.title: Empirical Risk and Loss Functions in Modeling
children.summary: Changing the TETA value alters the model, which in turn affects the cost function and predictions. The empirical risk is evaluated as the average loss across the dataset, specifically using L2 loss (mean squared error) and L1 loss (mean absolute error). The segment emphasizes the importance of understanding how these loss functions influence the final model, with a promise to explore the optimization of both functions in the next lecture.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And why is it going to change? Because when you change the TETA value, you're effectively changing the model. When you're changing the model, your cost function is going to be different because your predictions are going to be different. So think about this function as a function that evaluates the entire family of the lines that can pass through your data set. So when we go ahead and plug in for that loss function and plug in L2 loss, you can see that effectively the empirical risk is going to be 1 over N times the summation of Yi minus Yi i2, and we call that mean a squared error. And if you plug in L1 loss instead of this loss function into the empirical risk equation, this is going to give us 1 over N times the summation of absolute value of all of those residual values that we have. So, MSc versus MAE. In the next lecture, I'm going to come back and play with optimizing both of these functions. And I'm going to show you what's the difference between the final model that we'll get depending on which loss function that we use and which empirical response we get at the end. children.title: L2 Loss and Mean Squared Error in Regression
children.summary: L2 loss, or mean squared error (MSE), is used to calculate the residual square of each data point in a dataset, forming the empirical risk function. This method is known as least squares regression, as it aims to minimize the errors across the entire population. The average loss, or empirical risk, is assessed across all points in the dataset, with MSE defined as the average of the squared differences between actual and predicted values. Additionally, the segment briefly mentions L1 loss, or mean absolute error (MAE), as an alternative loss function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: For today's purpose, what I'm going to do is I'm going to say that I'm going to use L2 loss, meaning that I'm going to go ahead and calculate the residual square of each one of those data points in the dataset. And my entire empirical risk function is going to be MSE or mean squared error. Again, this is the mean and this is the squared error of all of those predictions that we have. This is YI, this is YI hat that we have based on the simple linear regression. What you know from data 8 is that you know that they called this delinear regression that they were teaching you, they called it least the squares regression. We also used exactly the same terminology here. Effectively, simple linear regression pattern or function together with the optimization function that is mean squared error is called least the scores regression because we're trying to find a function that minimizes these scores of the errors that we have over the entire population. children.title: Fitting Simple Linear Regression Models
children.summary: Fitting a model involves finding the optimal values for parameters theta 0 and theta 1 that minimize the cost function, specifically the mean squared error (MSE). This process requires calculating the derivatives of the cost function with respect to these parameters, setting them to zero to find the optimum points, which will yield the estimated parameters theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. The overall modeling process includes choosing a model, selecting a loss function, fitting the model, and evaluating its performance, with least squares regression being the focus for today.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So now down to the fun part. I said that if you have the two parts in the model, you have the model kind of Hadron or the template which in this case is simple linear regression and you know what kind of cost function you're using Then everything else is going to be easy fitting the model is going to be easy What I'll do is I'll basically tell you that what does it mean to fit the model when we say that we want to fit the model That means that I want to find a combination of theta 0 and theta 1 values that are giving me the minimum function or the minimum value for the right hand side of this msc function So in order to do so, what you know from your math courses is that I need to calculate the derivative of this function with respect to theta 0, set it to 0 with respect to theta 1, set it equal to 0, find those optimum points, and those optimum points, theta 0 and theta 1 are going to be exactly my theta 0 had on my theta 1 hat that I can use. children.title: Calculating Partial Derivatives for MSE
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of partial derivatives in machine learning, emphasizing the relevance of calculus and linear algebra. It introduces the objective function for minimizing Mean Squared Error (MSE) in a simple linear regression model, explaining that the MSE is calculated as the average of the squared differences between observed and predicted values. To find optimal values for the regression coefficients, the derivatives of the MSE are set to zero, leading to the optimality conditions necessary for model fitting.

children.title: Calculating Partial Derivative of MSE
children.summary: The segment discusses calculating the mean squared error (MSE) and its partial derivative with respect to theta zero. It begins with the definition of the residual, expressed as y_i minus y_i hat, and explains the process of taking the derivative while treating all other variables as constants. The lecturer emphasizes that the derivative of a summation can be simplified by applying the rule that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives. This leads to the formulation of the MSE as a summation term, facilitating the calculation of the derivative.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right? So this is the error inside the parentheses. What you're seeing is y i minus y i hat, which is the residual to the power of two that I'm calculating, which is mean a scored error that I want to calculate. So I'm going to take the derivative with respect to tetasera, which effectively what that means is that I'll do the normal derivative, but I'm just assuming that anything except for tetasera is going to be constant value, right? And there are a couple of steps that I need to do first. One is that I'm taking the derivative of a summation term, right? And we know that the derivative of sum is equal to sum of the derivatives. So I'm going to take out the coefficient and the summation out of the derivatives so that things are a little bit easier and cleaner. So moving this summation out is is going to give me 1 over n times the summation of pi from 1 up to n of the derivative of what I had, which is yi minus theta zero. children.title: Calculating Partial Derivatives of MSE
children.summary: The segment covers the calculation of the partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta_0 and theta_1. It explains that the derivative of a summation can be expressed as the summation of the derivatives and applies the chain rule to differentiate the MSE function. The discussion includes the expression (yi - theta_0 - theta_1 * xi)^2 and simplifies the derivatives, presenting the final expressions for the derivatives with respect to both parameters. Specifically, the derivative with respect to theta_0 is shown as negative 2/n times the summation of the differences between observed and predicted values, while the derivative with respect to theta_1 is derived using similar principles.

children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE with Respect to Parameters
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of calculating the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta_0 and theta_1. It begins by stating that the derivative of a summation can be expressed as the summation of the derivatives. The chain rule is applied to differentiate the outer function, which involves theta_0 raised to the power of 2. The coefficient is noted as 1/n, and the summation runs from i=1 to n, focusing on the expression (yi - theta_0 - theta_1 * xi)^2.

children.summary: The discussion focuses on calculating the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta_0 and theta_1. The derivative is derived by considering the function as a squared term and applying the chain rule. The result simplifies to a formula involving a summation of the differences between the observed values (yi) and the predicted values (theta_0 + theta_1 * x_i), multiplied by constants. The final expression for the derivative with respect to theta_0 is presented as negative 2/n times the summation of these differences.

children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE with Respect to Parameters
children.summary: The segment discusses calculating the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta_0 and theta_1. It emphasizes that the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives, and illustrates taking the derivative of the MSE formula. The process involves treating theta_1 as the variable of interest while considering other variables as constants. The chain rule is applied in the derivation, leading to a simplified expression for the derivative.

So we need to have both of the derivatives ready to go. So I'll do exactly the same thing that the derivative of sum is equal to some of the derivatives. I'm taking out the coefficient 1 over n and the summation out of the derivative. So it's going to give me 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of. Um... of the derivative of. Yes. Why I minus. That is 0 minus Teta 1 Xi is square with respect to Teta 1. Right. Same story here, right? So the story is that I'm taking the partial derivative with respect to tet 1. children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE Using Chain Rule
children.summary: The segment discusses taking the derivative of a function raised to the power of two with respect to theta one, applying the chain rule. The derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) is derived, showing that the derivative of the term involving the function is negative x_i. The simplification of constants and the application of the chain rule are emphasized, leading to a clearer understanding of the derivative process in the context of MSE.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But tet 1 is raised to the power of 2, right? And it's within a function. So if I want to take the derivative of this function. to the power of two with respect to, um, theta one, this is going to be equal to two times function times the derivative of function with respect to theta one. And that's exactly what I'll do. Uh, and I'm assuming that this is this function f that I'm calling. Um, so I'm going to apply the chain rule exactly. So one over n times the summation of i from one up to n of the function y i minus theta zero minus theta one x i. Now the derivative. So what is the derivative of that green term with respect to theta one? Yes. Negative x i is the derivative, right? So negative x i is my derivative right here. So I'm just going to go ahead simplify constants one more time. So here is going to give me, sorry, I missed it too. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE and Residuals
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to parameters theta zero and theta one. It emphasizes that the summation of residuals should equal zero at the optimum point of theta zero, indicating that the residuals cancel each other out. Additionally, it explains that the summation of residuals multiplied by the independent variable x_i should also equal zero, reinforcing the relationship between residuals and the optimization of the model parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's two times f times whatever. So this is going to be negative 2 over n of the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus minus theta zero minus theta one x i times x i. Right. Look at both sides. Look at that function f thing that's in common between them. So one is effectively telling me that if I go ahead and set this to be equal to zero, one, the left hand side is effectively telling me that the summation of all of the residuals should be equal to zero. Because that's residual. And that's why they were canceling each other out. That's why they couldn't be a cost function. Right? This is telling me that at the point that theta zero is optimum, the summation of residuals should be equal to zero. And then the right hand side is basically telling me that The summation of residuals times x i's should be equal to zero. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Estimating Optimal Parameters and Model Evaluation Techniques
children.summary: The segment discusses estimating optimal values for parameters theta zero hat and theta one hat using key equations involving residuals. The derivation process simplifies these equations to find conditions where derivatives equal zero, leading to optimal values and minimizing mean squared error (MSE). It details the calculation of theta zero hat as y bar minus the slope times x bar, and introduces an algebraic method for deriving theta one hat, incorporating adjustments to define the objective function. The relationship between observed values and predictor variables is established, culminating in a formula for theta one hat that involves standard deviations and correlation coefficients. Additionally, model evaluation techniques are emphasized, including data visualization and the computation of statistics like the correlation coefficient, which are critical before performing linear regression analysis.

children.title: Estimating Optimal Values and Derivation of Equations
children.summary: The segment covers the estimation of optimal values for parameters theta zero and theta one, referred to as theta zero hat and theta one hat, using two key equations. These equations involve the summation of residuals equating to zero and the summation of residuals multiplied by the predictor variable xi. The derivation of estimating equations is discussed, simplifying the original equations to find conditions where derivatives equal zero, leading to optimal values. The process also includes minimizing the mean squared error (MSE) through setting partial derivatives to zero, ultimately deriving the estimating equations that relate observed and predicted values.

children.title: Estimating Optimum Values of Theta
children.summary: Two equations with two unknowns, theta zero and theta one, are used to find their optimum values, denoted as theta zero hat and theta one hat. The process involves solving estimating equations where the summation of residuals equals zero and the summation of residuals multiplied by xi also equals zero. These equations are crucial for estimating the optimum theta values. The segment also mentions that a clean version of the equations will be provided in the slides.

children.title: Deriving Estimating Equations for Optimality Conditions
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of estimating equations used to find optimal values in a model. It begins with the equation set to zero and simplifies it into separate terms, including the summation of observed values and the parameters involved. The goal is to derive conditions where the derivatives equal zero, leading to two estimating equations: one relating to the difference between observed and predicted values, and the other involving the product of residuals and predictors.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's go ahead and do that. So these are the two equations. One over n times the summation of yi minus yi, i hat is equal to zero. And then the second equation is on the right hand side. What I'll do is I'll derive the first step. So deriving the first step means that what does it mean for this equation to be equal to zero? Is there any way that we can simplify it further? So what I'll do is I'll go ahead and try to... The compose all of these tree terms that are inside the parentheses and inside the summation into separate terms. Meaning that what I'll do is I'll say that this equation is equal to 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi. That's the first term. And then I'll stay minus 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of teta 0 hat. Right? And then minus... 1 over n times this dimension of i from 1 up to n of theta 1 hat x i equal to 0. children.title: Optimal Parameter Estimation Using Derivatives
children.summary: To find the optimal values of parameters theta 0 and theta 1, the partial derivatives of the estimating equations are set to zero. This process leads to the transformation of theta 0 and theta 1 into their optimal estimates, denoted as theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. The equations for estimating these parameters are derived from minimizing the mean squared error (MSE), which involves setting the derivatives of the MSE with respect to the parameters equal to zero.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Can someone tell me why my tetas right now turned into tetahats? Because it's optimized to zero. Remember in the previous slide when I was on the previous slide here. Actually, yeah, right here when I was driving all of these values, the tetah 0 and tetah 1, they were just normal tetah 0 and tetah 1 of the function. I actually derived it and calculated the partial derivative with respect to tetah 0, with respect to tetah 1. At the point that you actually set it equal to 0, which is what we have right here, I set this equal to 0, then tetah 0 will become tetah 0 hat, because that's the optimum theta 0. And theta 1 will become theta 1 hat, because I'm also setting this one equal to 0. So that's at the point that you're calculating the optimum and you're finding the exact optimum values by setting the partial derivatives equal to 0, that's when theta 0 and theta 1 will turn into theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. children.title: Estimating Equations and Optimality Conditions
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of finding optimal values for theta zero hat and theta one hat by setting derivatives equal to zero to obtain optimality conditions. It simplifies the average of y values to theta zero hat and explains that the second term in the equation also results in theta zero hat. The last term involves pulling out theta one hat from the summation since it is a constant. The goal is to solve two estimating equations to determine the best estimates for the parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Let me go back to this place that we were. So that's why I have theta 0 hat and theta one hat right here as I'm solving. And then at the end, we'll get to what is the actual value for theta zero hat and what is the actual value for theta one hat. So let me continue. What is the value? What is the simplified version of one over n times summation of yi values? That's why barn, that's average of why values. Right? The other thing, what is the simplified version of this? Teta 0 hat is a constant value. I'm summing it up n times, that this is going to be n times Teta 0 hat, and then I'm multiplying 1 over n. So this is equal to just teta zero hat. So the second term is teta zero hat. And then what is the last term? So the last term, there's no easy way to simplify the except for saying that I'm going to pull out theta one hat outside of the summation because it's a constant value. children.title: Derivation of Linear Regression Coefficients
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of the intercept coefficient (theta zero hat) in simple linear regression. It explains that the mean of the independent variable (x bar) is used in the equation, simplifying to y bar minus the product of the slope (theta one hat) and x bar. This reinforces the relationship previously established for calculating the y-intercept, indicating consistency in the derived equations for the regression model.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So then I'll have theta one hat times the summation of i from one up to n of x i's, which this one right here is also going to be x bar because that's the Sorry, there's one over n. One over n times the summation of x i's is going to be x bar. Again, setting it equal to zero, kind of cleaning up what I wrote here. y bar minus teta zero, hat minus. PEPTA 1 Times X bar is going to be equal to zero. Right? What does this tell you? This one is telling you that remember when I was calculating the y interest I'm always saying that teta zero hat is equal to y bar minus a slope times x bar. This is exactly that. This is deriving exactly the same equation for the teta zero hat that we had from before. just drive the true. The crazy sequence of defining the simple linear regression to going through the finding the cost function we chose mean a squared error as our cost function. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Finding Optimal Parameters and Deriving Estimators
children.summary: The discussion covers calculating optimal parameters theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat by setting partial derivatives to zero, with theta 0 hat derived as y bar minus the slope times x bar. An algebraic method is introduced for finding theta 1 hat, adjusting equations systematically to define the objective function. The derivation of theta 1 hat involves multiplying equation 1 by x bar to incorporate x i in equation 2, leading to a sum that equals zero. Further simplification reveals a relationship involving the differences between y_i and y bar, x_i and x bar, and the variance of x, culminating in a formula for theta 1 hat. The segment also discusses the correlation between standard deviations in modeling, expressing the left-hand side of an equation as the product of the correlation coefficient and the standard deviations of x and y, allowing the solution for theta 1 hat.

children.title: Finding Optimal Parameters in Estimating Equations
children.summary: The discussion focuses on calculating the optimal parameters theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat by setting partial derivatives to zero. The derived formula for theta 0 hat is y bar minus the slope times x bar. To find theta 1 hat, an algebraic method is introduced where equation 2 is adjusted by subtracting equation 1 multiplied by x bar. This approach is explained as a systematic way to define the objective function, although the specific derivation is not required for exams.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We calculated partial derivatives, we set partial derivatives to equal to zero in order to find out what is the optimum tetaheus zero valiant that's a one value. And then it just happens that if I simplify the first function or the first derivation, it's going to tell me that your theta 0 hat is going to be equal to y bar minus the slope times x bar, which is what we know from a slight tree of today's lecture. But now we systematically drive that and now we have a formal way of defining the objective function. The only thing that we need to now do is to figure out what is this theta 1 hat. But we still have two equations. We just looked at one of the equations. If we combine the two equations together, which I'm not going to try to derive it manually, but I'm going to walk you through the steps, is... It's going to help us to find out Teta 1 hat. The trick that we're going to use here is that we're going to take equation 2. We're going to subtract equation 1 times x bar from it. This is just an algebraic trick that you don't need to know and you should not be expected to know in the exams. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc

leaf_chunk.audio: We calculated partial derivatives, we set partial derivatives to equal to zero in order to find out what is the optimum tetaheus zero valiant that's a one value. And then it just happens that if I simplify the first function or the first derivation, it's going to tell me that your theta 0 hat is going to be equal to y bar minus the slope times x bar, which is what we know from a slight tree of today's lecture. But now we systematically drive that and now we have a formal way of defining the objective function. The only thing that we need to now do is to figure out what is this theta 1 hat. But we still have two equations. We just looked at one of the equations. If we combine the two equations together, which I'm not going to try to derive it manually, but I'm going to walk you through the steps, is... It's going to help us to find out Teta 1 hat. The trick that we're going to use here is that we're going to take equation 2. We're going to subtract equation 1 times x bar from it. This is just an algebraic trick that you don't need to know and you should not be expected to know in the exams.

children.title: Deriving Estimators from Equations
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of Teta 1 hat using estimating equations. It explains the multiplication of equation 1 with the average of x (x bar) to accommodate the x i term in equation 2. The speaker factors out the common term (y i - y i hat) and describes the resulting expression involving y i - y i hat and x i - x bar, leading to a sum that equals zero. The speaker also mentions substituting y i hat with its corresponding equation to further the derivation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But this is what I'm using in order to derive how we can find the Teta 1 hat. So if I go ahead and do the calculations, So what you're seeing is that this is equation 2 right here that we're seeing. This term right here is the equation 1 multiplied with x bar or the average of x. You can kind of guess why I multiplied it with x bar. This is because the equation 2 has that x i term dangling there and it just need to have something of the nature of x for to accompany it. And that's exactly what I'm using it. So what I can do right now is going to be I'm factoring out all of the y i's minus y i hats because that's the term that's in common between them. So then this is how this is going to turn out to be y i minus y i hat and then x i minus x x bar and then multiplied some up average it's equal to zero. I'm going to take one more step. I'm going to substitute y, y, i hat here with the equation for y, i hat. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Deriving Theta One from Estimating Equations
children.summary: The optimum value of y hat is expressed as theta zero plus theta one times x. Theta zero is derived from the equation y bar minus the product of theta one hat and x bar. This leads to a single equation where all variables are known except for theta one hat. Further simplification of this equation reveals a relationship involving the differences between y_i and y bar, x_i and x bar, and the variance of x, ultimately providing a formula for theta one hat.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the optimum y i hat is going to be theta zero plus theta one times x i. So I'm going to use that. What I'm going to use again is going to be I'm going to replace theta zero with the equation that we just derived. Right? The equation for theta zero was that it's y bar minus slope, which is set to 1 hat times x bar. So now what you're seeing is that I have one equation. All of the variables are known except for a teta one hat. And what I can do is I can just keep simplifying it in order to find out what is teta one hat from this equation. So if I keep simplifying, which I'm not going to go through the steps, it's going to give us this equation right here. So what is this equation? This equation right here is telling me that, uh, okay, so you have yi minus y bar, you have xi minus x bar, and the right hand side you have, teta 1 hat multiplied with the, with the variance of x. children.title: Correlation and Machine Learning Modeling
children.summary: The segment discusses the relationship between correlation and standard deviations in modeling. It explains that the left-hand side of a specific equation can be expressed as the product of the correlation coefficient (r) and the standard deviations of x and y. The derivation leads to the conclusion that one can solve for a parameter (theta one hat) using this relationship. Additionally, it emphasizes that machine learning practitioners define a mathematical objective function rather than relying on guesswork.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um, so kind of a reminder from what we know from correlation, you know that the correlation was equal to this equation right here, which sounds similar to what we have in the left hand side. So what we can do is we can say that the left-hand side right now is going to be equal to r times a standard deviation of x times a standard deviation of y because the only thing that this this equation is missing compared to this equation is these denominators right here. So the left-hand side of this equation is r times a standard deviation of x times a standard deviation of y. The left-hand side is my theta one hat times the variance or a standard deviation in the square of x. And then what's happening here is that I only have one unknown. I can directly solve it. Now that the one hat is going to be equal to our times the standard division of x over a standard division of y. This kind of sounds like magic, but now we formally define how actual machine learning people do modeling. They don't guess. They basically have a mathematical objective function that they define. children.title: Simple Linear Regression and Cost Function
children.summary: The segment defines the equation for simple linear regression, highlighting that the function varies based on the values of teta 0 and teta 1. It discusses the use of the cost function, specifically the mean squared error, and the calculation of partial derivatives to determine optimal values for teta 0 and teta 1, denoted as teta 0 hat and teta 1 hat. Key points include that the average of residuals equals zero, the summation of residuals equals zero, and the relationship between residuals and predicted variables, indicating that residuals are orthogonal to the predicted variables. Upcoming discussions will further explore these concepts.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: They define the family of functions or the model pattern, which is what we did, but define what is the equation for simple linear regression. But we said that, okay, depending on the tetasero and tetabone value, it's going to be a different function or a different line that we're mapping. How do we find out how good that function is with defined the cost function? We actually use minus squared error, we actually venture calculating partial derivatives in order to find out, Find out the optimum values for teta 0 and teta 1, which got us the teta 0 hat and teta 1 hat that we have. So this is where we are. What we got at the end was that was the two kind of final notes that I told you when I was doing the derivation. I said that the average of residuals is equal to zero or the summation of residuals is equal to zero. And I also said that the residual multiplied with the x i is equal to zero. I think on Tuesday of next week, we're going to talk about the fact that this means that the residuals are orthogonal to the predicted variables. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling X

children.title: Model Evaluation Techniques and Residual Analysis
children.summary: The discussion covers evaluating model performance in data analysis, emphasizing techniques such as visualizing data and computing statistics like the correlation coefficient, which must be acceptable before linear regression analysis. It includes calculating the mean squared error and its square root, the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), which is crucial for assessing model accuracy, with lower RMSE indicating better predictions. The segment highlights the importance of residual plots in evaluating linear regression models, where an ideal plot shows no patterns, suggesting a good fit. The evaluation process incorporates original data visualization, performance metrics like RMSE, and will lead to discussions on constant models and additional loss functions.

children.title: Model Evaluation Techniques in Data Analysis
children.summary: The discussion focuses on evaluating the performance of a model, which is the final step in the modeling process. Key techniques include visualizing data and computing statistics, such as the correlation coefficient, which must be acceptable before proceeding with linear regression analysis. Additionally, the mean squared error is calculated, along with its square root, to further assess model performance. The segment also touches on estimating equations, highlighting that for simple linear regression (SLR), residuals should average to zero and be orthogonal to the predictor variable.

children.title: Evaluating Model Fit and RMSE
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of calculating the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) to evaluate model performance. RMSE is derived from the mean squared error and is expressed in the original units of the data, making it meaningful for estimation. A lower RMSE indicates more accurate predictions. Additionally, the visualization of residuals is emphasized; a residual plot should not exhibit any patterns, indicating a good fit of the regression line to the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's called root minus the square root error. And the reason that we calculate the square root of it is because we want to turn the error into the original unit of the data. Because the error is in the second order. It's when we're calculating a square root of the errors, we were squaring the error. So the unit was in the squared units. So by taking a square root, we're going back to the original unit of the data. So that value is going to be meaningful if you're doing any estimation. The last thing that we will do, and this is what you will see in discussions, is we will visualize your residuals. So when we go ahead and visualize residuals, what we want to see is that if these are the four different data sets that I'm dealing with and I'm passing the regression line through them, you can kind of see that the regression line matches one or two of them perfectly, but it's not a suitable choice for the rest of them. If you go ahead and visualize the residual, meaning that on the y-axis, you plot the residuals and the x-axis, you plot x, we don't want this residual plot to have any kind of pattern. children.title: Evaluating Linear Regression with Residual Plots
children.summary: Residual plots are used to evaluate the appropriateness of linear regression models. An ideal residual plot shows no discernible pattern, indicating that the errors are randomly and equally distributed across the predictor variable. If a pattern is present, it suggests that linear regression may not be a suitable model. The evaluation process includes visualizing original data, computing statistics, using performance metrics like RMSE for simple linear least squares models, and visualizing residuals. Upcoming discussions will cover constant models and additional loss functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Which is what you're seeing in this first case that residual plot does not show any pattern, but you're seeing that residual plot absolutely shows the pattern, meaning that linear regression was not a good choice. Or residual plot, in this case also shows the pattern. also in this case shows the pattern, right? So what we want at the end is that once you build the residual plot, you really want to make sure that the errors are kind of randomly distributed and equally distributed across X and also across the residual kind of dimension. Your T's are going to more talk about this a little bit more in the discussion. Thank you for coming. We're going to talk about constant model and more loss functions on Thursday. Video: 1: v
